metadata.summary: Determine optimal pricing by anchoring on value delivered, analyzing competitors, and using Van Westendorp method with N≥30 customer research to design 3-tier structures.

content.title: Pricing Research
content.summary: A methodology for determining optimal pricing by combining value-capture math, competitor analysis, Van Westendorp willingness-to-pay research, and tier design. The rule: anchor on value delivered first (target 10-20% capture rate), map the competitive field, validate with customer research (N≥30 for statistical significance), then design 2-3 tiers with a clear anchor, main revenue tier, and entry option.

why.p: Pricing set by gut feel or competitor copying leads to chronic underpricing (leaving revenue on the table) or overpricing (losing customers). Value-based anchoring prevents the commodity race-to-the-bottom. The Van Westendorp method produces an empirically grounded acceptable price range rather than a single guess. Three tiers with a 2-3x jump between them exploit anchoring psychology to direct most customers to the mid-tier.

list.item: Pre-launch: setting initial price for a SaaS, course, template pack, or service.
Re-pricing: revenue-per-customer is below 10% of value delivered.
Designing a new tier (Pro / Enterprise) on top of an existing plan.
Choosing between subscription, usage-based, one-time, or hybrid models.
Fewer than 10 paying customers — insufficient signal; use competitor anchor + intuition and iterate.
Highly bespoke enterprise sales where price is negotiated per deal.
Marketplace or two-sided platforms where take-rate dynamics dominate.
Regulated pricing (healthcare, energy, telecom) where compliance frameworks apply instead.

section.name: value-and-models
section.p: How to quantify value delivered, apply the value-capture formula, select the right pricing model, and benchmark against competitors.

rule.statement: Quantify value delivered before looking at competitor prices; anchor on value to prevent pricing in a race to the bottom.
rule.rationale: Competitor-anchored pricing is a commodity strategy. Value-based anchoring reveals how much more or less than the market your product justifies.

rule.statement: Apply the value-capture formula: Max Price = (Value Delivered x Capture Rate) - Switching Cost. Typical capture rate: 10-20% for enabling tools, 30-50% for revenue-generating tools.
rule.rationale: The 10-20% rule is folklore — not a calibrated number. High-leverage revenue-generating tools capture much more. Use the formula as a floor, not a ceiling.

example.title: Value quantification example
example.code: Tool saves 5 hours/week User time value: $50/hour Monthly value: 5 x $50 x 4 = $1,000/month Capture 10%: $100/month max price Capture 20%: $200/month max price

list.item: Time saved — hours per week x hourly rate x 4 weeks.
Money saved — direct cost reduction per month.
Money earned — new revenue enabled per month.
Risk reduced — estimated cost of prevented problems.
Quality improved — cost of doing this worse without the tool.

rule.statement: Choose the pricing model that scales with the customer's value consumption; subscription for ongoing use, usage-based for variable consumption, one-time for discrete deliverables.
rule.rationale: Misaligned pricing models create friction at renewal. Customers who use a subscription product only once per quarter will churn; usage-based retains them.

list.item: Subscription — best for sticky ongoing-value tools (most SaaS).
One-time — best for discrete deliverables: courses, templates, guides.
Usage-based — best for variable consumption: API calls, storage, seats added.
Freemium — best for PLG products where network effects or viral spread matters.
Tiered — best when customer segments have clearly different needs and budgets.

rule.statement: Build a pricing matrix for at least 5 competitors with entry, mid, and pro tier prices and feature gating before finalizing your own structure.
rule.rationale: The competitive context sets customer expectations and defines what "normal" looks like in the market. Pricing outside the range requires explicit positioning justification.

antipattern.description: Passing a single scraped pricing page snapshot to an analyzer agent and treating it as current.
antipattern.reason: Competitor pricing is A/B tested per visitor and changes frequently. Always pass freshly-scraped HTML; stale data misprices the product.

section.name: tiers-and-validation
section.p: Rules for designing pricing tiers, the Van Westendorp willingness-to-pay method, validation approaches, and antipatterns in tier construction.

rule.statement: Design 3 tiers: entry (low-friction acquisition), pro (main revenue target), enterprise (anchor at top). The middle tier should be where you want most customers.
rule.rationale: Three options exploit anchoring psychology. The top tier makes the mid-tier look reasonable; the bottom tier captures price-sensitive leads who upgrade over time.

rule.statement: Price jumps between tiers should be 2-3x; each tier needs one compelling "must-have" feature unavailable in the tier below.
rule.rationale: Without a clear differentiator, customers stay on the lowest tier. The 2-3x jump is a rough guideline — test your specific market. For solopreneur SaaS, $9/$19/$39 often outperforms $9/$27/$81.

rule.statement: Always offer annual pricing at 16-20% discount; never omit annual for products targeting retention over acquisition.
rule.rationale: Annual plans halve churn and improve cash flow. Customers who pay annually are more invested and use the product more.

antipattern.description: Adding a "Custom / Enterprise" tier to the public pricing page without any enterprise customers.
antipattern.reason: It signals unachieved scale. Enterprise prospects see it and call; you have no process for them. Add this tier when you have actual enterprise sales motion.

rule.statement: Ask the 4 Van Westendorp questions with at least 30 same-segment respondents before quoting an "optimal price".
rule.rationale: Below N=30, the intersection point is noise. Agents compute 4-decimal-place "optimal prices" from N=5 interviews — reject these results explicitly.

list.item: Q1: "At what price would this be too expensive to consider?" (Too Expensive)
Q2: "At what price would this be expensive but worth considering?" (Expensive)
Q3: "At what price would this be a good deal?" (Cheap)
Q4: "At what price would this be too cheap to trust?" (Too Cheap)

section.p: Optimal price = intersection of "Too Cheap" and "Too Expensive" curves. Acceptable range = between "Cheap" and "Expensive".

list.item: Pre-launch pricing page — measure clicks and signups by tier before building.
A/B test prices — different cohorts; measure conversion; require statistical significance before concluding.
Launch at higher price — easier to discount than to raise prices later.
Grandfather pricing — keep early adopters at original price when raising; goodwill outweighs marginal revenue from churn.

rule.statement: Any price change greater than 15% requires human review before publishing; automated repricing can cause customer-trust damage that surveys won't predict.
rule.rationale: Price anchoring is a psychological contract. Breaking it without communication generates churn and negative word-of-mouth that outweighs revenue gain.

section.name: agent-workflow-and-gotchas
section.p: Run as a 4-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (haiku): scrape the pricing matrix for ≥5 competitors (entry/mid/pro) plus their feature gating. Stage 2 (sonnet): compute value-capture math from the founder's value claims; flag if capture > 25% (probably overpriced) or < 5% (underpriced). Stage 3 (sonnet): design 3 candidate tier structures and label psychology (anchor, decoy, growth-path). Stage 4 (opus): write a Pricing Research Report with confidence level + a validation plan (A/B test, pricing-page intent, customer-development calls). Founder picks the launch price.

list.item: Van Westendorp requires N ≥ 30 same-segment respondents to be statistically meaningful. Agents quote "Optimal price = $X" from N=5 interviews — that's noise, not signal.
"10-20% of value delivered" capture rate is industry-folklore, not a calibrated number. Anchoring there can leave 50%+ on the table for high-leverage tools.
Competitor pricing pages are increasingly opaque ("Contact us") or A/B tested per visitor. Agents that scrape once get a stale snapshot.
The "2-3x price jump between tiers" rule fails for low-priced solopreneur SaaS (e.g., $9 → $19 → $39 outperforms $9 → $27 → $81).
Annual discount math (~17% for "2 months free") cannibalizes monthly cohort if billing is not gated behind a clear upgrade event.
Pricing-page A/B tests have huge variance; agents conclude "price X wins" from non-significant lifts.
LLMs trained on 2022 data quote stale competitor prices. Always pass freshly-scraped HTML into the analyzer agent.
Agents will happily compute Van Westendorp on N=4 responses and quote a 4-decimal-place "optimal price". Reject results below N=30 with explicit error.
Capture-rate of "10-20%" is repeated as if it's a law. It's not — high-leverage tools (revenue-generators) capture 30-50%+ in practice.
LLM-generated tier features tend to be vague ("priority support", "advanced analytics"). Force a specific feature list with measurable units.
"Decoy" tiers should be designed deliberately, not as an afterthought. Agents pad the top tier with random features; require explicit rationale.
Annual-discount math: agents say "12 months for the price of 10 = 17% off" but charge 16.7% — bias toward the cleaner 20%.
Human checkpoint: any price change > 15% should be reviewed before publishing — automated reprice can cause customer-trust damage that survey data won't predict.
Currency / locale: agents default to USD. International pricing requires PPP adjustment and local-tax handling (VAT, GST) — flag explicitly.
Anchor on value before competitors. Competitor-anchored pricing is a race to the bottom in commodity categories.
Charge in the currency / units that scale with the customer's value (per-seat for collaboration, per-event for analytics, per-GB for storage).
Always offer annual at ~16-20% discount — it improves cash flow and halves churn on average.
Three tiers is the sweet spot: anchor (decoy at top), main (where you want most customers), entry (low-friction acquisition).
Don't include the 4th "Custom" tier on the public page if you have no enterprise customers yet — it signals scale you don't have.
Re-validate every 6 months. Markets shift; AI-augmented competitors compress prices fast.
Grandfather existing customers when raising prices. Public goodwill > marginal revenue from churn.
Test pricing-page copy more than the price itself — value framing typically moves conversion more than the dollar number within ±20%.

template.purpose: Full report template: value analysis, competitor matrix, customer research, recommended structure, validation plan.
Lightweight 1-page pricing check: competitor benchmark, value estimate, customer feedback, recommendation.
Python script: reads CSV of 4-question survey responses, outputs optimal price point and indifference price.
